original_hypothesis.title: Any Token Holder Can Deploy ERC20 via withdraw() Bypassing Subject's onlyTokenSubject
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: withdraw() calls _deployERC20 directly when no ERC20 exists, bypassing the onlyTokenSubject modifier on _mint(). Any holder of a curves token can trigger ERC20 deployment with default name/symbol (or whatever metadata was pre-set). Once deployed, ERC20TokenAlreadyMinted prevents the subject from choosing different metadata.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This bypasses the subject's exclusive control over ERC20 naming. An attacker could force deployment with default 'Curves'/'CURVES' naming before the subject sets desired name/symbol.

proof_of_signal.pattern: withdraw() calls _deployERC20 directly without onlyTokenSubject check; _mint has the modifier but withdraw bypasses _mint

preconditions: Attacker holds at least 1 curves token for the subject
ERC20 not yet deployed for this subject
Subject has not yet set name/symbol (or set them but not yet minted)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Any holder triggers deployment; subject loses ability to choose custom metadata
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No onlyTokenSubject check in withdraw's ERC20 deployment path

analysis_questions: Can the subject change name/symbol after ERC20 deployment?
What metadata is used — pre-set by subject or defaults?
Does this break symbolToSubject uniqueness in any way?
validation_requirements: Buy token as non-subject, call withdraw before subject mints, verify ERC20 deploys with defaults

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) Subject creates curve (buys first token). 2) Subject hasn't set name/symbol yet. 3) Attacker buys 1 token. 4) Attacker calls withdraw(subject, 1). 5) ERC20 deploys with 'Curves'/'CURVES' default. 6) Subject cannot change — ERC20TokenAlreadyMinted.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: No direct financial loss, but reputational damage and loss of subject control over token identity

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test for ERC20 deployment via withdraw by non-subject

validation_result.mechanism: Access Control Bypass in ERC20 Deployment
validation_result.impact_summary: Any token holder can force the deployment of a subject's ERC20 token with default metadata, permanently locking the subject out of choosing a custom name and symbol.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The `withdraw` function in `Curves.sol` allows users to convert their internal curves tokens into ERC20 tokens. If the ERC20 token for a given subject has not been deployed yet, `withdraw` automatically deploys it. However, `withdraw` lacks the `onlyTokenSubject` modifier, meaning any user who holds at least one token can trigger this deployment. If the subject has not yet set a custom name and symbol via `setNameAndSymbol`, the `withdraw` function assigns the default name ('Curves') and symbol ('CURVES'). Once the token is deployed, the `externalCurvesTokens[curvesTokenSubject].token` state is set. Any subsequent attempt by the subject to call `setNameAndSymbol` or `mint` will revert with `ERC20TokenAlreadyMinted()`. This completely bypasses the subject's exclusive right to configure their token's identity.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The code clearly shows that `withdraw` is a `public` function without access restrictions regarding who can trigger the ERC20 deployment. It checks if `externalToken == address(0)` and directly calls `_deployERC20`. In contrast, the `mint` function, which is intended for the subject to deploy their token, correctly uses the `onlyTokenSubject` modifier. This discrepancy allows the bypass.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The `withdraw` function is public and can be called by anyone holding a balance. It checks if the ERC20 token is deployed, and if not, it sets default metadata and deploys it via `_deployERC20`. This bypasses the `onlyTokenSubject` restriction intended for token deployment.

Once the token is deployed by an attacker via `withdraw`, `externalCurvesTokens[curvesTokenSubject].token` is no longer `address(0)`. The subject is permanently prevented from setting their desired name and symbol because this function will revert with `ERC20TokenAlreadyMinted()`.

execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: An attacker buys 1 token for a subject's curve. The attacker calls `withdraw(subject, 1)`. The function checks `externalToken == address(0)` which is true. It assigns `DEFAULT_NAME` and `DEFAULT_SYMBOL` because the subject hasn't set them yet. It calls `_deployERC20`, which deploys the token and updates `externalCurvesTokens[subject].token`. Later, when the subject tries to call `setNameAndSymbol` or `mint`, the transaction reverts.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario is highly realistic and requires minimal capital (the cost of 1 token). 1) A subject creates a curve by buying the first token. 2) Before the subject calls `setNameAndSymbol` or `mint`, an attacker buys 1 token. 3) The attacker immediately calls `withdraw(subject, 1)`. 4) The ERC20 token is deployed with default metadata ('Curves'/'CURVES'). 5) The subject is permanently locked out of customizing their token's metadata.
validation_result.impact_assessment: While there is no direct theft of funds, the impact on the protocol's usability and the subject's reputation is significant. Token subjects lose control over their token's identity, which is a core feature of the protocol. This constitutes a permanent denial of service for the `setNameAndSymbol` functionality for the affected subject.
recommended_mitigations: Modify the `withdraw` function to revert if the ERC20 token has not been deployed yet, rather than deploying it automatically. Require the subject to explicitly deploy the token via `mint` before any user can withdraw.
Alternatively, if automatic deployment is desired, only allow the `curvesTokenSubject` to trigger the deployment path within `withdraw`.
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Initialize the Curves contract. 2. Have User A (subject) buy the first token to initialize their curve. 3. Have User B (attacker) buy 1 token of User A's curve. 4. Have User B call `withdraw(User A, 1)`. 5. Verify that the ERC20 token is deployed with default metadata. 6. Have User A call `setNameAndSymbol(User A, 'Custom', 'CST')` and assert that it reverts with `ERC20TokenAlreadyMinted()`.
